title: Performance Optimization
description: Python performance from profiling to Staff+ optimization: cProfile, __slots__, array, multiprocessing, asyncio, C extensions, and JIT compilation.

sections.title: 1. Profiling — Measure Before You Optimize
sections.description:
Never guess about performance. Use cProfile for CPU profiling and tracemalloc for memory. Profile in production-like environment only.

cProfile: deterministic profiler. python -m cProfile -s cumulative script.py shows total time per function. pstats filters and sorts results. snakeviz visualizes flame graphs.

py-spy: sampling profiler for production. No code changes needed. Attaches to running process.

timeit: micro-benchmarking. Only for comparing small code snippets. Don't use it to benchmark I/O.

FAANG angle: 'Your service is CPU-bound. How do you optimize it?' — profile first. cProfile shows the bottleneck. 90% of the time, the fix is algorithmic, not language-level.
sections.codeBlock:
# === cProfile usage ===
# Terminal:
# python -m cProfile -s cumulative my_script.py

import cProfile, pstats

def slow_function():
    result = []
    for i in range(10000):
        result.append(sum(range(i)))
    return result

profiler = cProfile.Profile()
profiler.enable()
slow_function()
profiler.disable()

# Save and analyze
stats = pstats.Stats(profiler)
stats.sort_stats('cumtime').print_stats(10)

# === timeit for micro-benchmarks ===
import timeit

# Compare list comprehension vs map
setup = 'nums = list(range(1000))'
lc = timeit.timeit('[x**2 for x in nums]', setup=setup, number=10000)
mp = timeit.timeit('list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))', setup=setup, number=10000)
print(f'list comp: {lc:.4f}s, map: {mp:.4f}s')

# === py-spy (production safe) ===
# pip install py-spy
# sudo py-spy record -o profile.svg --pid 12345
# sudo py-spy top --pid 12345

sections.title: 2. Data Structure Choices — Memory and Speed
sections.description:
The right data structure is worth 100x the wrong optimization. Know these tradeoffs cold.

list vs deque: list O(1) append/pop at end, O(n) at front. deque O(1) at both ends.
__slots__: ~40% memory reduction, ~15% attribute speedup.
array.array: C-native contiguous memory — use for numeric data instead of list of floats.
set/dict: O(1) average, but 2-3x memory overhead vs list.
tuple: if immutable, tuple is cheaper than frozenset for small collections.

FAANG angle: 'You have 10 million (int, float) records. How do you store them to minimize memory?' — array.array('f') for floats + separate array for ints. Not list[tuple[int, float]].
sections.codeBlock:
import sys
from collections import deque
import array

# list vs deque for queue
queue = deque([1, 2, 3])
queue.append(4)        # O(1)
queue.popleft()        # O(1) vs list.pop(0) is O(n)

# __slots__ comparison
class PointSlots:
    __slots__ = ('x', 'y')
    def __init__(self, x, y): self.x, self.y = x, y

class PointDict:
    def __init__(self, x, y): self.x, self.y = x, y

print('slots:', sys.getsizeof(PointSlots(1, 2)))     # 56 B
print('dict:', sys.getsizeof(PointDict(1, 2)))       # 72 B

# array.array for numeric data
# list of floats: 8 B (pointer) + 24 B (float object) = 32 B per element
floats_arr = array.array('d', [1.0, 2.0, 3.0])  # 8 B per element
print('array size:', sys.getsizeof(floats_arr))   # 104 B + 8*3

# tuple for small immutable collections
# tuple: 56 B overhead, frozenset: 224 B overhead
print('tuple:', sys.getsizeof((1, 2, 3)))          # 64 B
print('frozenset:', sys.getsizeof(frozenset({1, 2, 3})))  # 224 B

sections.title: 3. Concurrency — Threading, Multiprocessing, Async
sections.description:
threading: for I/O-bound tasks. GIL prevents parallelism for CPU work. Use concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor.

multiprocessing: for CPU-bound tasks. New process per worker. Overhead ~500ms per fork. Use Pool or concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor.

asyncio: for high-concurrency I/O (1000s of connections). asyncio.run(), await asyncio.sleep(0) to yield. Use asyncio.gather() for concurrent awaits.

PEP 703 (nogil): Python 3.13+ has experimental free-threading. Removes GIL for CPU-parallel threads. Not yet production-ready but is the future.

FAANG angle: 'Your web service makes 10 downstream API calls per request. What concurrency model?' — asyncio with aiohttp. 1000 concurrent connections per thread, vs 10 per thread with threading.
sections.codeBlock:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor
import asyncio
import time

# ThreadPoolExecutor (I/O bound)
def io_task(url):
    import requests
    return requests.get(url).status_code

urls = ['http://example.com'] * 20
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(io_task, urls))

# ProcessPoolExecutor (CPU bound)
def cpu_task(n):
    return sum(range(n * 1_000_000))

with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(cpu_task, [1, 2, 3, 4]))

# asyncio (high-concurrency I/O)
async def fetch(session, url):
    import aiohttp
    async with session.get(url) as resp:
        return resp.status

async def main():
    import aiohttp
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch(session, f'http://example.com/{i}')
                 for i in range(100)]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

results = asyncio.run(main())

# asyncio vs threading: thread overhead ~8 MB per thread,
# asyncio task overhead ~200 bytes per coroutine

sections.title: 4. C Extensions, Cython, and JIT
sections.description:
For the last mile of performance, call C directly.

ctypes: stdlib, call C libraries. Simple but slow per-call overhead.
CFFI: faster than ctypes. Generates C-compatible wrappers.
Cython: Python-to-C compiler. Static type declarations (cdef) give C speed. 10-100x speedup for numeric loops.
Numba: JIT compiler for numeric Python. @numba.jit decorator compiles loops to machine code. Best with NumPy.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your team's Python data processing is 100x too slow. What do you do?' — profile first. If the bottleneck is loops over numeric data, use Numba or rewrite in Cython. If it's I/O, use asyncio. If it's algorithmic, rewrite in C++ or Rust. Don't reach for C unless you've exhausted Python-level optimization.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Numba JIT ===
import numba
import numpy as np

@numba.jit(nopython=True)
def monte_carlo_pi(n):
    inside = 0
    for i in range(n):
        x = np.random.random()
        y = np.random.random()
        if x*x + y*y <= 1.0:
            inside += 1
    return 4.0 * inside / n

print(monte_carlo_pi(10_000_000))  # ~3.141, ~10x faster than pure Python

# === Cython example (in .pyx file) ===
# def sum_range(int n):
#     cdef int i
#     cdef int total = 0
#     for i in range(n):
#         total += i
#     return total

# === ctypes (call C from Python) ===
# import ctypes
# libc = ctypes.CDLL('libc.dylib')
# libc.printf(b'hello\n')

# === Simple optimization: local variables ===
def slow_sum(n):
    items = range(n)
    total = 0
    for i in items:
        total += i
    return total

def fast_sum(n):
    # Python local variable lookup is faster than global
    range_local = range
    total = 0
    for i in range_local(n):
        total += i
    return total

sections.title: 5. Staff+ Patterns — System-Wide Optimization Strategy
sections.description:
At Staff+ level, optimization means designing for performance from the start, not bolt-on fixes.

Latency budgets: Every service has a latency SLO. Allocate budget per component. Profile the tail latency (p99) — that's what users experience, not the average.

Bottleneck analysis: CPU-bound? Memory-bound? I/O-bound? Network-bound? Each requires a different strategy. Amdahl's Law: speedup is limited by the serial portion. Optimizing a component that's 10% of runtime can only give 10% improvement.

Caching strategy: In-memory (lru_cache), local (Redis/memcached), CDN. Cache invalidation is harder than caching — use TTL-based invalidation when possible.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design a low-latency prediction service running ML models' — batch requests, pre-allocate tensors, use model parallelism across GPUs, cache common inputs, pre-warm model caches, and serve from replicated shards with consistent hashing.
sections.codeBlock:
# Latency budget example (commented, not executable)
# Service SLO: p99 < 200ms
# Allocation:
#   network (client -> service):   20ms
#   auth + rate limit:             10ms
#   feature extraction:            50ms
#   model inference:               80ms
#   post-processing:               20ms
#   network (service -> client):   20ms
#   Total:                        200ms

# Bottleneck analysis: Amdahl's Law
# Speedup = 1 / ((1 - P) + P/S)
# where P = proportion of runtime that's parallelizable
#       S = speedup of that portion
# Even with infinite parallelism, if 10% is serial,
# max speedup = 1 / 0.1 = 10x

# Caching strategy (multi-level)
class CacheStrategy:
    def __init__(self):
        self.l1 = {}  # in-process dict (fastest, process-scoped)
        self.l2 = None  # Redis client (medium, service-scoped)

    def get(self, key):
        result = self.l1.get(key)
        if result is not None:
            return result
        # fall through to Redis
        result = self.l2.get(key)
        if result is not None:
            self.l1[key] = result  # warm L1
        return result

# Request batching for ML inference
def batch_predict(model, requests):
    # Combine multiple requests into one tensor for GPU efficiency
    batch = [r['features'] for r in requests]
    outputs = model(batch)
    return dict(zip([r['id'] for r in requests], outputs))

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• cProfile docs
  • asyncio docs
  • Numba — JIT compiler
  • Cython — C extensions for Python
  • High Performance Python (2nd ed.) by Micha Gorelick and Ian Ozsvald

details:
Optimization workflow: (1) Profile — measure, don't guess. (2) Identify the bottleneck (CPU/Memory/I/O/Network). (3) Choose the right data structure (100x improvement possible). (4) Apply concurrency model (thread/process/async) matching the bottleneck type. (5) Only then reach for C/Numba/Cython.

Common mistakes: Optimizing without profiling; using threads for CPU-bound work; using multiprocessing for I/O-bound; choosing asyncio for CPU-bound; premature micro-optimization (hoisting locals, using __slots__ before proving the bottleneck).

Staff+ expectations: Design systems with latency budgets and bottleneck analysis. Know when to reach for asyncio, multiprocessing, Numba, or C extensions. Build multi-level caching strategies. Understand Amdahl's Law and the limits of parallelism.
